status_at_import: 10 articles and 4 tools merged via word-families-enhancements modules.

user_scenarios: A crossword solver knows a clue answer starts with PRE and has seven letters.
A teacher wants examples of words beginning with un- for a spelling mini-lesson.
A writer needs alliterative options that start with ST or TR.
A word-game player wants possible rack ideas but still needs to verify game validity.
tips: Start with the shortest confirmed prefix, then add letters only when the list is too broad.
Use length filters before scanning long rare-word lists.
For prefix study, compare un-, re-, pre-, and dis- to see how meanings differ.
When the start is known but one internal letter is unknown, use Word Finder pattern mode.
Use Words Containing if the same letters can appear anywhere instead of only at the start.

user_scenarios: A crossword solver knows an answer ends with TION and has eight letters.
A student wants examples of words ending in -ness for a suffix assignment.
A songwriter is checking spelling endings before moving to a rhyme tool.
A game player wants possible -ed or -ing extensions but must still validate the play.
tips: Use common endings such as -ing, -ed, -er, -ly, and -tion as starting points.
For crosswords, set the exact answer length with matching min and max values.
For writing, use the list to brainstorm, then choose the word that fits meaning and rhythm.
For rhyme, check pronunciation separately because spelling can mislead.
When the ending plus an internal cluster matters, use Words Containing as a second step.

user_scenarios: A crossword solver has _QUA_ in the middle of an answer.
A student wants examples containing EIGH for spelling practice.
A word-game player sees a QUA board cluster and wants possibilities.
A writer wants words with a repeated visual pattern for a title or exercise.
tips: Use three or more letters for cleaner results when possible.
If the cluster is at the start, switch to Words Starting With for stricter output.
If the cluster is at the end, switch to Words Ending With.
For rack letters that can move around, use Word Finder Letters mode.
Pair contains searches with common endings to learn productive spelling patterns.

user_scenarios: A player has seven letters and wants to know possible words before choosing a board move.
A crossword solver knows C _ A _ E and needs five-letter candidates.
A puzzle player wants to compare game-list and broad-dictionary output.
A teacher uses pattern mode to demonstrate how fixed letters narrow possibilities.
tips: Use Letters mode for rack-style searches and Pattern mode for fixed slots.
Turn on scores only after you have a manageable candidate list.
Try Game list first for word-game practice, then Full list for vocabulary exploration.
For crosswords, enter every known position instead of only the letters you remember.
After finding candidates, check meaning and board placement before using a word.
